.ddx/beads.jsonl:135:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer running `go test`, my test runs do not leave behind registered projects in my real `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`. When a machine has already been polluted, I can prune it with one command. The server sweep is aggressive enough that obvious test-dir pollution does not survive a restart.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Fix A — test isolation enforced by CI.**\n   - Every existing test that instantiates `ServerState` or boots a server helper is migrated to an isolated state dir. No test writes to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`.\n   - A lint test (Go or shell) parses `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` (and any other directories that wire up `ServerState`) and fails if it finds a construction that does not pass an explicit isolated state path or override `XDG_DATA_HOME` first. Test cases added for both the allowed-pattern and the disallowed-pattern.\n\n2. **Fix B — sweep recognizes test-dir pollution.**\n   - `ServerState.migrate` drops entries whose `Path` matches any of:\n     - prefix `/tmp/`, `/private/tmp/`, `/var/folders/`\n     - regex `/Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\\d+/` anywhere in the path\n   - Missing paths matching these patterns are dropped on first sweep (no 24h tombstone).\n   - Reachable paths matching these patterns are still dropped (they are never real projects — the test dir may exist transiently but is never the user's registered project).\n   - Unit test seeds a fixture state with one real project, one `/tmp/TestFoo123/001` missing, one `/tmp/TestBar456/002` still on disk, and one `/private/tmp/TestMac789/001`; asserts only the real project survives.\n\n3. **Fix C — pruning CLI.**\n   - `ddx server state prune [--dry-run] [--state \u003cpath\u003e]` applies the Fix B rules to an on-disk state file.\n   - Writes `server-state.json.bak-\u003cYYYYMMDDTHHMMSS\u003e` before overwriting.\n   - Prints summary: `Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)`. Dry-run prints the summary without writing.\n   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.\n\n4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**\n   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. (Implementation detail: sweep on startup + skip tombstoned in the hot loop.)\n   - If Fix A+B are in place, a fresh `go test ./...` run followed by a server restart produces a state file with ≤ 20 projects on a typical dev machine. Asserted via a CI job that runs the tests, starts the server briefly, inspects the state file, and fails if test-dir patterns are present.\n\n5. **Backward compatibility.**\n   - The prune CLI is safe to run on an existing polluted state file. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.\n   - On upgrade, a note is logged: \"Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file.\"\n\n6. **Cross-reference.**\n   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"a101e215a0b34590951f51340d2f25c07ff1c5cc","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:44:13.806406362Z","description":"## Observed\n\n`~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` on a developer machine contains **7,343 registered projects**. Composition:\n\n- **6,936 named \"001\"** — all are Go test temp dirs like `/tmp/TestAgentCapabilitiesCommandJSON1451829170/001`, `/tmp/TestAgentCheckSuccess3666443068/001`.\n- **344 named \"002\"** — same pattern in tests that use `002/`.\n- **~60 real projects** (ddx, helix, axon, plus per-branch worktrees).\n- Only 135 of the 6,936 are marked unreachable; the rest are \"alive\" because their `/tmp` dir still exists or because `LastSeen` is recent.\n- State file size: 1.8 MB.\n\n## Root cause\n\n1. Go tests that exercise the server (or anything that wires up a `ServerState`) call `RegisterProject` with a temp-dir path. The state file they write to is **not isolated** — it defaults to the shared XDG location (`serverAddrDir()` at `cli/internal/server/server.go:67`) unless the test explicitly overrides `XDG_DATA_HOME` or constructs state via the `tc.StateDir` injection path.\n2. There is no test-side deregistration on teardown. Temp dirs get cleaned up by `t.TempDir()`'s own finalizer, but the project entry in the production state file stays.\n3. The reachability sweep (`ServerState.migrate` at `cli/internal/server/state.go:85-148`) tombstones missing-path entries and drops them 24h later. With thousands of entries being added per test run, the rate of addition outpaces the 24h GC.\n\n## Perf amplifier (cross-cutting)\n\nBoth `GetBeadSnapshots` (`state_graphql.go:49-105`) and `GetAgentSessionsGraphQL` (`state_graphql.go:476-489`) iterate every registered project per request. With N=7343 that's ~7k `os.Stat` + `store.ReadAll` attempts per bead-list or sessions query, mostly against dead paths. This is part of why `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) feel slow in practice even before their own N·M bugs are considered.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nThree independently-landable fixes:\n\n### Fix A — Test isolation (highest leverage)\n\n- Audit every test that calls `ServerState` / `RegisterProject` / boots a server. Each one must point at an isolated state dir (`t.TempDir()` + `XDG_DATA_HOME` override, or direct `tc.StateDir` injection).\n- Add a linting test: parse `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` for constructions of `ServerState` or HTTP server test helpers and assert each uses an isolated state dir. Fail CI otherwise.\n- This is the real fix. The rest is damage mitigation.\n\n### Fix B — Smarter sweep (defense in depth)\n\n- Sweep: any path under `/tmp`, `/private/tmp`, `/var/folders/*/T/` (macOS test temp root), or matching `/tmp/Test*` should be treated as a **candidate test-dir project** and dropped immediately on first sweep that finds it missing — no 24h tombstone.\n- Sweep should also drop entries where `Path` contains any `Test[A-Z]\\w+\\d+` segment (the Go testing convention), regardless of whether the dir currently exists. These are never real projects.\n- Unit test: seed a state with mixed real + fake-test-dir entries, run sweep, assert only the real ones remain.\n\n### Fix C — Manual cleanup CLI\n\n- `ddx server state prune` — read state file, apply Fix B's rules, write backup to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json.bak-\u003ctimestamp\u003e`, write cleaned state, print summary (X dropped, Y kept).\n- Useful for recovery on machines that have already accumulated pollution — like the reporter's. Keep even after Fix A lands.\n- Dry-run flag prints the summary without writing.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Moving state to SQLite.\n- Per-user project management UI. (Follow-up if the projects list becomes a real operator surface.)\n- Renaming \"001\" away from a confusing default. .ddx/beads.jsonl:194:{"archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-15T01:13:31Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3766264","closing_commit_sha":"604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0","created_at":"2026-04-15T01:11:17.497753676Z","description":"The embedded 'agent' harness can complete a task successfully (commit produced, tests pass) but then fail to terminate, getting stuck in a compaction retry loop with compaction always failing. The parent execute-loop never observes task_succeeded and hangs forever.\n\n## Observed\n\nRunning 'ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --harness agent --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next' against axon-43ed769b (axon auth-core-types bead):\n\n1. qwen3-coder-next ran the task correctly, producing commit 91d4bdb with 636 LOC of types + 109 green tests + clippy clean. The commit was present in the worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-axon-43ed769b-20260415T005652-ebe7a16a.\n\n2. Instead of terminating, the agent kept generating events. After ~20 minutes the session log was 33 MB (8461+ events, 249 messages) and stuck in:\n\n    {\"type\":\"compaction.start\",...}\n    {\"type\":\"compaction.end\",...,\"data\":{\"messages_after\":249,\"messages_before\":249,\"no_compaction\":true,\"success\":false}}\n    {\"type\":\"compaction.start\",...}\n    {\"type\":\"compaction.end\",...,\"data\":{\"messages_after\":249,\"messages_before\":249,\"no_compaction\":true,\"success\":false}}\n    ...\n\n3. Parent ddx process in __futex_wait. Had to SIGTERM the parent (PID 4141811) and manually fast-forward-merge the worktree commit to land the work.\n\n4. A1 (previous bead) ran the same harness/model and terminated correctly in ~14 min with identical-looking commit shape. The difference was bead size: A2's output was a single 633-line Rust file, vs A1's 350-line SQL + 290-line test file. A2 didn't exceed what A1 did — so the context-limit explanation doesn't fully account for it.\n\n## Expected\n\n- After the agent commits and has nothing more to do, it should emit task_succeeded and terminate\n- Compaction should either succeed (messages_after \u003c messages_before) or bail out cleanly, not retry indefinitely\n- If the context genuinely cannot be compacted, the agent should report a structured failure and preserve the commits, not spin\n\n## Scope\n\n- Detect mid-turn compaction returning success=false repeatedly and bail with a structured 'compaction_stuck' status\n- Bound the total number of compaction attempts in a single turn\n- Ensure task termination happens immediately after a successful commit + no pending tool calls\n- Add a watchdog at the execute-loop level: kill the child if no commit+progress event is observed within a budget\n\n## Evidence\n\n- Session log: /Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)\n- Rescued commit: 91d4bdb on axon master\n- Parent process: PID 4141811 (killed)\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- [ ] Compaction retry loop is bounded and fails structurally\n- [ ] Post-commit termination path is deterministic\n- [ ] Watchdog detects and terminates stuck sessions with a preserved-commit outcome\n- [ ] Regression test reproduces the stuck state (seeded session with large no-op tool calls)","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"merged onto current tip\nresult_rev=604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0\nbase_rev=9943221220ec53cace570413e07dc6e809a5995a","created_at":"2026-04-15T01:39:52.60471501Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"success"},{"actor":"audit-reviewer","body":"The DDx-side circuit breaker in commit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 is a caller-layer workaround. The root cause (compaction driver producing 8461+ no-op events without a native bound) belongs upstream in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent.\n\nFiled upstream bead agent-e5228424 in /home/erik/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl with:\n- The bug description\n- Pointer to DDx workaround commit as defense-in-depth\n- Requested native fix (bounded compaction attempts + structured ErrStuck)\n- Regression test requirement (seeded stuck-state reproduction)\n\nWhen the upstream fix lands and DDx bumps the agent library version, the caller-side circuit breaker can be removed.","created_at":"2026-04-15T03:46:00.187180985Z","kind":"note","source":"upstream companion bead filed 2026-04-15","summary":"Upstream native fix requested at github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent: bead agent-e5228424 (compaction loop native stuck detection). DDx circuit breaker is defense-in-depth; can be removed once upstream fix lands."}],"execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-15T01:39:31.55993889Z","id":"ddx-25a41f51","issue_type":"bug","labels":["helix","area:agent","area:exec","kind:bug","gap:stability"],"owner":"ddx","priority":0,"session_id":"eb-e80e780a","status":"closed","title":"bug: embedded agent stalls in post-commit compaction loop","updated_at":"2026-04-15T03:46:00.206978601Z"}
.ddx/beads.jsonl:203:{"acceptance":"- [ ] Option A, B, or C selected and documented in an ADR or design note under the ddx repo\n- [ ] Implementation lands with migration path for existing .ddx/beads.jsonl files (no lost bead history)\n- [ ] Concurrent writes from 2+ workers on the same machine produce a deterministic, semantically correct tracker state\n- [ ] Concurrent writes from 2+ machines (via ddx server or shared remote) produce a deterministic, semantically correct tracker state\n- [ ] Claim races are resolved by exactly one winner; losers see a structured 'already_claimed' error\n- [ ] Crash recovery: a claimed bead whose worker died returns to unclaimed after the heartbeat timeout (existing mechanism continues to work)\n- [ ] Existing beads.jsonl format continues to work as a read-only snapshot for offline tooling (jq queries, etc.)\n- [ ] Unit + integration tests covering the concurrency scenarios above\n- [ ] Migration test: apply the upgrade to axon's current beads.jsonl (748+ beads on sindri side, 30+ new on eitri side) and verify no semantic loss","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-14T12:20:46.447948869Z","description":"Fourth child of ddx-b7ee0d6f. .ddx/beads.jsonl is a git-tracked mutable ledger. Multiple writers on different machines or in parallel worker processes produce divergent files that require manual textual merge. git merge can sometimes append-merge JSONL cleanly if each side touches disjoint lines, but when both sides touch the same bead (claim / close / add-event), the merge is semantically broken even when git reports it as resolved.\n\n## Evidence\n\nOn axon, eitri and sindri both wrote to beads.jsonl independently for 14 days. Local has 134 commits touching the tracker; origin has 336. When I tried to rebase my planning commit onto origin, git reported conflicts in .ddx/beads.jsonl — and these conflicts would have been 'resolved' by git's textual merge logic even when the semantic outcome is wrong (e.g. two machines claiming the same bead with different claimed-machine values).\n\nThe existing execute-loop help text says:\n\n    By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background\n    worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current\n    process.\n\nSo a ddx server EXISTS and is supposed to coordinate. But .ddx/beads.jsonl is still the ledger, which means the server is not the source of truth — it's just a submission surface. The git-tracked file is authoritative, and that's what's racing.\n\n## Options (pick one during implementation)\n\n**A. Server-authoritative tracker.** When the ddx server is running, it owns beads.jsonl. All reads and writes go through the server's API. The git-tracked file becomes a periodic snapshot for backup/offline use, not the source of truth. Workers on multiple machines talk to a shared server.\n\n**B. CRDT append-only log.** Replace the current representation with an append-only log keyed by (machine_id, bead_id, sequence, timestamp). Each writer appends to its own partition; readers compute the effective state by merging partitions with a conflict-resolution rule (last-writer-wins per (bead_id, field), or better). git textual merge of the partitioned log is always safe because writers never share lines.\n\n**C. Per-bead lock files with atomic rename.** Each bead mutation acquires a lock file (beads/\u003cid\u003e.lock), writes beads/\u003cid\u003e.json atomically via rename, releases the lock. The JSONL aggregate is regenerated from per-bead files. Git tracks per-bead files, not the aggregate. Safe across local parallelism but not across machines without network coordination.\n\n**Recommendation**: A is the cleanest if the ddx server is already deployed as authoritative infrastructure. B is the best fit for the current decentralized model. C is a stopgap that solves intra-machine parallelism but not multi-machine drift.\n\n## Scope note\n\nDo NOT conflate this bead with the fetch/push/rebase triad (ddx-bd42f640, ddx-83ec1430, ddx-6aa50e57). Those three fix the git-level divergence; this bead fixes the tracker-level divergence. Both are needed — the triad without tracker safety would still produce semantically-conflicting beads.jsonl merges even when the underlying git history is linear.\n\n## Test cases\n\n- Two workers on the same machine claim different beads concurrently: both claims persist correctly\n- Two workers on different machines claim different beads concurrently: both claims persist correctly (requires network coordination)\n- Two workers attempt to claim the same bead: one wins, the other sees 'already claimed' and picks a different one\n- Recovery from crash between claim and CloseWithEvidence: the bead returns to unclaimed after a heartbeat timeout\n- Tracker state is deterministic from any valid ordering of concurrent writes (CRDT option)","id":"ddx-2791dc4f","issue_type":"bug","labels":["bug","execute-loop","tracker","concurrency"],"parent":"ddx-b7ee0d6f","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-820c201","status":"closed","title":"execute-loop: .ddx/beads.jsonl concurrent-write safety","updated_at":"2026-04-14T14:45:05.880884507Z"}
.ddx/beads.jsonl:226:{"acceptance":"AC1. ProviderTimeout is configurable per-project via .ddx/config.yaml under agent.routing or agent.endpoints — e.g. agent.endpoints[provider].request_timeout_seconds — and the current 15-minute hardcoded constant becomes a default that can be overridden upward (or removed entirely) per provider.\n\nAC2. ProviderTimeout is configurable per-tier via the model-overrides path: cheap tier on a known thinking model (qwen3.6, deepseek-r1, etc.) defaults to a longer cap (e.g. 60 min or unlimited). Cloud chat models keep the 15-min default.\n\nAC3. The idle-read timeout (DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 min) is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense; documentation in cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go and the user-facing config docs explain the relationship between idle-read and wall-clock-request timeouts and when each fires.\n\nAC4. When ProviderTimeout fires, the error message names the exact timeout setting and the path to override it (e.g. 'request_timeout=15m exceeded; configure agent.endpoints.\u003cname\u003e.request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml').\n\nAC5. execute-loop / execute-bead --request-timeout DURATION CLI flag exists for one-off overrides during debugging.\n\nAC6. Default for known-thinking-model surfaces (provider.type=lmstudio AND model name matches qwen3.6/qwen-r1/deepseek-r1 etc., or some other declared 'thinking' attribute) is bumped to a sensible value (e.g. 60 min) without requiring per-project config.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T20:58:18Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"932496","closing_commit_sha":"99375eaf330ea13838d40d2176d193ae3cd3453b","created_at":"2026-04-29T18:50:46.475563514Z","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30\n  const DefaultProviderRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute\n\nThis is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every provider regardless of model class. It is set on every service path:\n  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout\n  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout\n\nThe comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'\n\nBut that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:\n  const DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute\n\nThe 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.\n\nConcrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):\n\n1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)\n   - status: no_changes\n   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)\n   - log: 'WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err=\"provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s\" delay=1s'\n   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.\n\n2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)\n   - All 3 hit 'provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt\n   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each\n\nWhy thinking models hit this:\nqwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning models can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the harness considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the harness is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.\n\nWhy arbitrary timeouts are not helping:\n\na) Same value for cloud providers (claude, codex) and local providers (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking models routinely need \u003e15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.\n\nb) No way to opt out of the cap per provider, per model, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.\n\nc) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.\n\nd) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking model the timeout doesn't mean 'broken provider', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{\"resolved_provider\":\"claude\",\"resolved_model\":\"sonnet\",\"fallback_chain\":[]}","created_at":"2026-04-29T21:15:43.162977264Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx .ddx/beads.jsonl:1260:{"acceptance":"ddx bead show ddx-1210706d --json no longer reports closing_commit_sha=9653820049db7edebe0374431544b1b8a8dbae88, and the provenance field used by FEAT-019 replay is documented or tested to identify the original governed-work diff rather than a later metadata-only tracker commit","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:05:03Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2142103","closing_commit_sha":"2b3e824d812ac23d2d3ac588da00d57cfda01601","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:58:44.196668651Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: .ddx/beads.jsonl:1\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: Commit 34b87b9 repoints ddx-1210706d.closing_commit_sha to 9653820049db7edebe0374431544b1b8a8dbae88 because that SHA is a pure tracker-only commit. But FEAT-019 replay uses closing_commit_sha as the original commit boundary: it checks out the parent of that SHA and compares against that commit's diff. For ddx-1210706d, that means replay now uses e51936a as the base state but compares against 9653820's one-line metadata diff, so the replay/audit surface no longer represents the actual tracker-governance work that bead governed, including the concerns.md taxonomy change mentioned in the bead acceptance. The last-commit fix removes the mixed-commit problem but replaces it with a metadata-only provenance target that cannot reconstruct the original governed-work diff.\nSuggested fix: Either clear ddx-1210706d.closing_commit_sha so replay falls back to sessionless/manual semantics for this backfilled bead, or introduce an explicitly documented and tested provenance model that distinguishes implementation diff provenance from later tracker-close metadata commits before storing a pure metadata SHA in closing_commit_sha.","id":"hx-63ac1232","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":".ddx/beads.jsonl","status":"closed","title":"correctness: stop ddx-1210706d replay provenance from pointing at metadata-only commit","updated_at":"2026-04-09T23:07:24.624928924Z"}
.ddx/beads.jsonl:1304:{"acceptance":"Either ddx bead show ddx-5448b39c --json no longer lists area:bead, or docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md declares bead in the Area Labels table so concern matching for that label is explicit","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T22:36:34Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2028312","closing_commit_sha":"5da08385781e424a3b85ace407dad1885d162e19","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:17:41.180034453Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: .ddx/beads.jsonl:4\nCategory: drift\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new open bead ddx-5448b39c is labeled area:bead, but docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md does not declare bead in its Area Labels table. Concern resolution relies on declared area labels to decide which practices apply, so this new bead is outside the documented taxonomy and cannot be matched deterministically.\nSuggested fix: Either change ddx-5448b39c to use only declared area labels, or update docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md to declare area:bead and explain how bead-scoped work should match active concerns.","id":"hx-84c89316","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":".ddx/beads.jsonl","status":"closed","title":"drift: resolve undeclared area:bead label on ddx-5448b39c","updated_at":"2026-04-09T22:36:50.964421381Z"}

Source material (consume in order):
1. /tmp/ddx-axon-backend-plan.md — full integration plan with Axon's external interface mapped + GraphQL pivot + locked decisions
2. Cross-repo tracking bead axon-82b6f7b2 — full DDx-on-axon adoption checklist (links to all required axon-side capabilities)

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Axon already supports ts-net per its serve.rs (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags). Reuses ADR-006.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.
5. Wire transport: GraphQL only via genqlient-generated Go client. NOT gRPC. Subscriptions over WebSocket for live updates.

Cross-repo dependencies (tracked in axon-82b6f7b2):
- axon-05c1019d (P2): GraphQL pattern-query support — needed for single-query ddx bead ready/blocked. Day 1 ships with two-phase fallback.
- FEAT-013 secondary indexes (P1 Draft in axon): needed for status-field query performance at >1000 beads.
- FEAT-017 schema evolution (P1 Draft in axon): needed for clean DDx schema upgrades over time. Day 1 ships with DDx-side lazy migration on read.
- ts-net auth: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon. No coordination needed.
- GraphQL CRUD + subscriptions: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon.
- Multi-namespace/database: ALREADY SHIPPED.

Adoption gates (per axon-82b6f7b2):
- Alpha: requires axon-05c1019d + everything already-shipped. Acceptable to ship with table-scan perf.
- Beta: requires FEAT-013 (indexes) for >1000-bead performance.
- GA: requires FEAT-013 + FEAT-017 + axon-05c1019d all landed.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: DDx-side lazy migrate on read until FEAT-017 lands; switch to axon-native then.
- ddx bead ready Day 1: two-phase GraphQL query.
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR.

Output: TD-<NNN>-axon-bead-backend.md with sections for: GraphQL schema mapping (DDx bead → Axon entity); operation mapping (DDx CLI → GraphQL queries/mutations/subscriptions); deployment + config; concurrency model (OCC via expectedVersion); migration plan; test plan; cross-repo coordination notes (links to axon-82b6f7b2 + FEAT-013/017).

.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/manifest.json:12:    "title": "Evaluate and adopt a real backend for the bead tracker (axon primary, bd fallback)",
.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/manifest.json:13:    "description": "The current .ddx/beads.jsonl backend (atomic JSONL writes wrapped in flock; events accumulating per-bead; archive split per ADR-004) is showing strain:\n\n1. Multi-worker contention via the git tracker-commit step (see ddx-da11a34a — the file lock around git add/commit is a v1 patch, not the real fix)\n2. Events array per bead is unbounded and dominates archive size\n3. Cross-machine concurrency is not addressed (Story 14 federation will require it)\n4. The current model bakes one process-local store + git-as-replication; doesn't compose cleanly with a real database\n\nTwo backend paths under consideration:\n\n**Axon (PRIMARY, per user inclination)**: integrate as the first DDx-side consumer of axon. User owns the stack; tight integration possible; proves axon's value as a substrate. Requires authoring an axon backend implementing the bead schema's storage contract (per ADR-004 / SD-004 — collection abstraction, claim semantics, atomic writes, JSONL interchange compatibility for bd/br).\n\n**bd / doltdb (FALLBACK)**: delegate to bd which already speaks doltdb (git-for-data). Trivial integration; downside is tracking an external codebase as a hard dependency.\n\nThis epic frames the evaluation, designs the integration, prototypes, and migrates. Children break down the work. The lock-contention patch (ddx-da11a34a) remains relevant as stability scaffolding while this plays out — when axon ships and is adopted, the JSONL+git path may retire entirely (or stay as a portable interchange backend per FEAT-004's bd/br semantics).",
.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "1. TD authored for the axon-backend integration (data model mapping, claim/lock semantics, archive policy, multi-machine concurrency story). 2. Axon-backend implementation lands behind a feature flag or selectable backend in cli/internal/bead/backend.go. 3. Migration tool moves an existing .ddx/beads.jsonl + .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl into the axon backend without data loss. 4. Existing chaos_test.go suite passes against the axon backend. 5. bd fallback documented (single artifact under docs/) showing how to wire bd as the backend if axon adoption stalls. 6. Performance: queue ops (ready/blocked/show) at parity or better than JSONL on the current 1100-bead archive scale.",

.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/prompt.md:3:    <title>Evaluate and adopt a real backend for the bead tracker (axon primary, bd fallback)</title>
.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/prompt.md:5:The current .ddx/beads.jsonl backend (atomic JSONL writes wrapped in flock; events accumulating per-bead; archive split per ADR-004) is showing strain:

1. Multi-worker contention via the git tracker-commit step (see ddx-da11a34a — the file lock around git add/commit is a v1 patch, not the real fix)
2. Events array per bead is unbounded and dominates archive size
3. Cross-machine concurrency is not addressed (Story 14 federation will require it)
4. The current model bakes one process-local store + git-as-replication; doesn't compose cleanly with a real database

Two backend paths under consideration:

**Axon (PRIMARY, per user inclination)**: integrate as the first DDx-side consumer of axon. User owns the stack; tight integration possible; proves axon's value as a substrate. Requires authoring an axon backend implementing the bead schema's storage contract (per ADR-004 / SD-004 — collection abstraction, claim semantics, atomic writes, JSONL interchange compatibility for bd/br).

**bd / doltdb (FALLBACK)**: delegate to bd which already speaks doltdb (git-for-data). Trivial integration; downside is tracking an external codebase as a hard dependency.

This epic frames the evaluation, designs the integration, prototypes, and migrates. Children break down the work. The lock-contention patch (ddx-da11a34a) remains relevant as stability scaffolding while this plays out — when axon ships and is adopted, the JSONL+git path may retire entirely (or stay as a portable interchange backend per FEAT-004's bd/br semantics).
.ddx/executions/20260503T051603-07c1516a/prompt.md:8:1. TD authored for the axon-backend integration (data model mapping, claim/lock semantics, archive policy, multi-machine concurrency story). 2. Axon-backend implementation lands behind a feature flag or selectable backend in cli/internal/bead/backend.go. 3. Migration tool moves an existing .ddx/beads.jsonl + .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl into the axon backend without data loss. 4. Existing chaos_test.go suite passes against the axon backend. 5. bd fallback documented (single artifact under docs/) showing how to wire bd as the backend if axon adoption stalls. 6. Performance: queue ops (ready/blocked/show) at parity or better than JSONL on the current 1100-bead archive scale.

Source material (consume in order):
1. /tmp/ddx-axon-backend-plan.md — full integration plan with Axon's external interface mapped + GraphQL pivot + locked decisions
2. Cross-repo tracking bead axon-82b6f7b2 — full DDx-on-axon adoption checklist (links to all required axon-side capabilities)

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Axon already supports ts-net per its serve.rs (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags). Reuses ADR-006.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.
5. Wire transport: GraphQL only via genqlient-generated Go client. NOT gRPC. Subscriptions over WebSocket for live updates.

Cross-repo dependencies (tracked in axon-82b6f7b2):
- axon-05c1019d (P2): GraphQL pattern-query support — needed for single-query ddx bead ready/blocked. Day 1 ships with two-phase fallback.
- FEAT-013 secondary indexes (P1 Draft in axon): needed for status-field query performance at >1000 beads.
- FEAT-017 schema evolution (P1 Draft in axon): needed for clean DDx schema upgrades over time. Day 1 ships with DDx-side lazy migration on read.
- ts-net auth: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon. No coordination needed.
- GraphQL CRUD + subscriptions: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon.
- Multi-namespace/database: ALREADY SHIPPED.

Adoption gates (per axon-82b6f7b2):
- Alpha: requires axon-05c1019d + everything already-shipped. Acceptable to ship with table-scan perf.
- Beta: requires FEAT-013 (indexes) for >1000-bead performance.
- GA: requires FEAT-013 + FEAT-017 + axon-05c1019d all landed.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: DDx-side lazy migrate on read until FEAT-017 lands; switch to axon-native then.
- ddx bead ready Day 1: two-phase GraphQL query.
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR.

Output: TD-<NNN>-axon-bead-backend.md with sections for: GraphQL schema mapping (DDx bead → Axon entity); operation mapping (DDx CLI → GraphQL queries/mutations/subscriptions); deployment + config; concurrency model (OCC via expectedVersion); migration plan; test plan; cross-repo coordination notes (links to axon-82b6f7b2 + FEAT-013/017).

.ddx/executions/20260502T230833-36ef6add/prompt.md:5:The current .ddx/beads.jsonl backend (atomic JSONL writes wrapped in flock; events accumulating per-bead; archive split per ADR-004) is showing strain:

1. Multi-worker contention via the git tracker-commit step (see ddx-da11a34a — the file lock around git add/commit is a v1 patch, not the real fix)
2. Events array per bead is unbounded and dominates archive size
3. Cross-machine concurrency is not addressed (Story 14 federation will require it)
4. The current model bakes one process-local store + git-as-replication; doesn't compose cleanly with a real database

Two backend paths under consideration:

**Axon (PRIMARY, per user inclination)**: integrate as the first DDx-side consumer of axon. User owns the stack; tight integration possible; proves axon's value as a substrate. Requires authoring an axon backend implementing the bead schema's storage contract (per ADR-004 / SD-004 — collection abstraction, claim semantics, atomic writes, JSONL interchange compatibility for bd/br).

**bd / doltdb (FALLBACK)**: delegate to bd which already speaks doltdb (git-for-data). Trivial integration; downside is tracking an external codebase as a hard dependency.

This epic frames the evaluation, designs the integration, prototypes, and migrates. Children break down the work. The lock-contention patch (ddx-da11a34a) remains relevant as stability scaffolding while this plays out — when axon ships and is adopted, the JSONL+git path may retire entirely (or stay as a portable interchange backend per FEAT-004's bd/br semantics).
.ddx/executions/20260502T230833-36ef6add/prompt.md:8:1. TD authored for the axon-backend integration (data model mapping, claim/lock semantics, archive policy, multi-machine concurrency story). 2. Axon-backend implementation lands behind a feature flag or selectable backend in cli/internal/bead/backend.go. 3. Migration tool moves an existing .ddx/beads.jsonl + .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl into the axon backend without data loss. 4. Existing chaos_test.go suite passes against the axon backend. 5. bd fallback documented (single artifact under docs/) showing how to wire bd as the backend if axon adoption stalls. 6. Performance: queue ops (ready/blocked/show) at parity or better than JSONL on the current 1100-bead archive scale.

.ddx/executions/20260502T145627-e580dc5b/prompt.md:5:The current .ddx/beads.jsonl backend (atomic JSONL writes wrapped in flock; events accumulating per-bead; archive split per ADR-004) is showing strain:

1. Multi-worker contention via the git tracker-commit step (see ddx-da11a34a — the file lock around git add/commit is a v1 patch, not the real fix)
2. Events array per bead is unbounded and dominates archive size
3. Cross-machine concurrency is not addressed (Story 14 federation will require it)
4. The current model bakes one process-local store + git-as-replication; doesn't compose cleanly with a real database

Two backend paths under consideration:

**Axon (PRIMARY, per user inclination)**: integrate as the first DDx-side consumer of axon. User owns the stack; tight integration possible; proves axon's value as a substrate. Requires authoring an axon backend implementing the bead schema's storage contract (per ADR-004 / SD-004 — collection abstraction, claim semantics, atomic writes, JSONL interchange compatibility for bd/br).

**bd / doltdb (FALLBACK)**: delegate to bd which already speaks doltdb (git-for-data). Trivial integration; downside is tracking an external codebase as a hard dependency.

This epic frames the evaluation, designs the integration, prototypes, and migrates. Children break down the work. The lock-contention patch (ddx-da11a34a) remains relevant as stability scaffolding while this plays out — when axon ships and is adopted, the JSONL+git path may retire entirely (or stay as a portable interchange backend per FEAT-004's bd/br semantics).
.ddx/executions/20260502T145627-e580dc5b/prompt.md:8:1. TD authored for the axon-backend integration (data model mapping, claim/lock semantics, archive policy, multi-machine concurrency story). 2. Axon-backend implementation lands behind a feature flag or selectable backend in cli/internal/bead/backend.go. 3. Migration tool moves an existing .ddx/beads.jsonl + .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl into the axon backend without data loss. 4. Existing chaos_test.go suite passes against the axon backend. 5. bd fallback documented (single artifact under docs/) showing how to wire bd as the backend if axon adoption stalls. 6. Performance: queue ops (ready/blocked/show) at parity or better than JSONL on the current 1100-bead archive scale.

Source material (consume in order):
1. /tmp/ddx-axon-backend-plan.md — full integration plan with Axon's external interface mapped + GraphQL pivot + locked decisions
2. Cross-repo tracking bead axon-82b6f7b2 — full DDx-on-axon adoption checklist (links to all required axon-side capabilities)

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Axon already supports ts-net per its serve.rs (Tailscale mode + LocalAPI whois + role tags). Reuses ADR-006.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.
5. Wire transport: GraphQL only via genqlient-generated Go client. NOT gRPC. Subscriptions over WebSocket for live updates.

Cross-repo dependencies (tracked in axon-82b6f7b2):
- axon-05c1019d (P2): GraphQL pattern-query support — needed for single-query ddx bead ready/blocked. Day 1 ships with two-phase fallback.
- FEAT-013 secondary indexes (P1 Draft in axon): needed for status-field query performance at >1000 beads.
- FEAT-017 schema evolution (P1 Draft in axon): needed for clean DDx schema upgrades over time. Day 1 ships with DDx-side lazy migration on read.
- ts-net auth: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon. No coordination needed.
- GraphQL CRUD + subscriptions: ALREADY SHIPPED in axon.
- Multi-namespace/database: ALREADY SHIPPED.

Adoption gates (per axon-82b6f7b2):
- Alpha: requires axon-05c1019d + everything already-shipped. Acceptable to ship with table-scan perf.
- Beta: requires FEAT-013 (indexes) for >1000-bead performance.
- GA: requires FEAT-013 + FEAT-017 + axon-05c1019d all landed.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: DDx-side lazy migrate on read until FEAT-017 lands; switch to axon-native then.
- ddx bead ready Day 1: two-phase GraphQL query.
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR.

Output: TD-<NNN>-axon-bead-backend.md with sections for: GraphQL schema mapping (DDx bead → Axon entity); operation mapping (DDx CLI → GraphQL queries/mutations/subscriptions); deployment + config; concurrency model (OCC via expectedVersion); migration plan; test plan; cross-repo coordination notes (links to axon-82b6f7b2 + FEAT-013/017).

.ddx/executions/20260429T211547-5226e7f2/prompt.md:89:+    "description": "Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30\n  const DefaultProviderRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute\n\nThis is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every provider regardless of model class. It is set on every service path:\n  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout\n  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout\n\nThe comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'\n\nBut that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:\n  const DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute\n\nThe 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.\n\nConcrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):\n\n1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)\n   - status: no_changes\n   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)\n   - log: 'WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err=\"provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s\" delay=1s'\n   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.\n\n2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)\n   - All 3 hit 'provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt\n   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each\n\nWhy thinking models hit this:\nqwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning models can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the harness considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the harness is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.\n\nWhy arbitrary timeouts are not helping:\n\na) Same value for cloud providers (claude, codex) and local providers (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking models routinely need \u003e15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.\n\nb) No way to opt out of the cap per provider, per model, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.\n\nc) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.\n\nd) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking model the timeout doesn't mean 'broken provider', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.",

.ddx/executions/20260429T205818-1f396b1e/prompt.md:5:Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30
  const DefaultProviderRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute

This is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every provider regardless of model class. It is set on every service path:
  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout
  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           ProviderTimeout: DefaultProviderRequestTimeout

The comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'

But that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:
  const DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute

The 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.

Concrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):

1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)
   - status: no_changes
   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)
   - log: 'WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err="provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s" delay=1s'
   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.

2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)
   - All 3 hit 'provider request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt
   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each

Why thinking models hit this:
qwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning models can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the harness considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the harness is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.

Why arbitrary timeouts are not helping:

a) Same value for cloud providers (claude, codex) and local providers (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking models routinely need >15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.

b) No way to opt out of the cap per provider, per model, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.

c) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.

d) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking model the timeout doesn't mean 'broken provider', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.

.ddx/executions/20260429T131728-ca327f53/prompt.md:5:When a local execute-bead/execute-loop process exits abnormally (killed, OOM, parent shell terminated, container crash) the worker entry can remain in the in-memory registry forever. `ddx agent workers` lists it as state=running with elapsed_ms continuously advancing, even though no PID exists.

Reproducing today (axon repo): worker local-20260429T030828-075a28c5 (bead hx-5f4d4ea1, harness=agent, model=qwen3.6) started at 03:08 UTC, has logged elapsed=10+ hours, and `ps -ef | grep execute-bead` shows nothing. The git worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-hx-5f4d4ea1-* has only the checkpoint commit (no actual work commits), confirming the worker died early.

Available paths to clean up are all unsatisfying:
1. `ddx agent workers stop --bead hx-5f4d4ea1` returns 'no matching workers' (because the matcher only finds live workers)
2. `ddx agent workers stop <worker-id>` returns server error 400 'worker not running' (same contradiction — the registry says running, the stopper says not running)
3. `systemctl --user restart ddx-server` works but kills every other active worker on the box, including ones for other repos that are doing real work
4. Manual edit of server-state.json risks corruption and isn't documented

The bead itself wasn't blocked (status=open, owner=null) so the registry noise is mostly cosmetic — it pollutes `ddx agent workers` output and confuses operators trying to triage the queue. But over weeks of use these accumulate (saw 14+ stale entries from prior sessions on this same box, with ages 134h–268h).

2. `ddx agent workers stop <worker-id>` returns server error 400 'worker not running' (same contradiction — the registry says running, the stopper says not running)

.ddx/executions/20260428T223637-9c85ab80/prompt.md:5:Standing optimization goal captured 2026-04-15: get the most work done for the least cost, and continuously improve our ability to measure and tune that trade-off.

## Operating principles

1. Cheap models do the work. Local (vidar-coder / qwen3-coder-next / other LM Studio hosts), then budget-tier cloud (claude haiku / gpt-5.4-mini), then standard (claude sonnet / gpt-5.4), then smart (claude opus / gpt-5.4 high-effort). Start cheap; escalate on failure.
2. Strong models review. Every closed bead gets an opus review against its acceptance. Rejected reviews reopen with specific fixes.
3. Deterministic fixes are the top rung of the ladder. When LLMs keep failing on a mechanical task, apply the fix manually; capture as input for routing policy.
4. Measurement feeds optimization. Track per-bead which model attempted, how long, whether it passed review, what failure mode if any.

## Existing bead tie-ins (helix review-loop epic from parallel agent)

- ddx-a317208c exec: post-merge review agent on-by-default in execute-loop
- ddx-dc231144 exec: tier-based auto-escalation driven by resource discovery
- ddx-fdf031c9 exec: ddx bead review <id> — template a review prompt
- ddx-44084b3e exec: ddx bead reopen <id> — atomic review-driven reopen
- ddx-518b2690 exec: ddx agent run --output text — plain result extraction

## Existing bead tie-ins (mine)

- ddx-bd0691fe Epic: surface ddx-agent routing visibility (providers/models/check/route-status commands — prereq for routing policy)
- ddx-bfc1aeaf Visibility 8: capture routing decisions in bead evidence (kind:routing) — feeds the measurement database

## New child beads (to be filed under this epic)

The beads below are scope for this epic. File as children when we are ready to work each one. NOT pre-filed to avoid blocking the current Svelte+GraphQL migration.

1. Per-bead cost aggregation: extend bead evidence schema to record tokens-in/tokens-out/cost-usd per attempt; add ddx bead metrics aggregate command that rolls up per-bead totals.
2. Ladder success-rate analytics: ddx agent metrics tier-success — query last N closed beads, report success rate at each tier (vidar-coder, claude-sonnet, claude-opus, deterministic-fix).
3. Prompt-context pre-filter: shrink the prompt for cheap-tier attempts — omit the full plan doc and include only the bead description + acceptance + 2 most relevant files.
4. Failure-mode taxonomy: document the known failure modes (vidar long-context hang, read-loop stall, ship-unbuilt-code, opus-shape-miss, etc.) as bead evidence kinds so measurement can aggregate by mode.
5. Review-rejection telemetry: aggregate opus-review outcomes (APPROVE / APPROVE_WITH_EDITS / REJECT) per originating tier so we know where the shape gaps are.
6. Dashboard page for the metrics (requires Svelte migration Stage 4 to land first).

## Why this is captured as an epic not a memory-only goal

Memory is read; epics are tracked. Future sessions should see this in ddx bead list and be able to file child beads against it as we discover new measurement needs or optimization opportunities. The memory file is the principle; this epic is the backlog.

.ddx/executions/20260425T073541-4f46941d/prompt.md:11:## 2026-04-22 — User directives on fixture size and storage strategy

Two standing instructions from the user that apply to this bead and to all other perf beads (`ddx-ad0db8fd`, `ddx-2ceb02fa`, and any spawned from efficacy/providers work):

1. **Minimum fixture size: 1,000s of records.** The original acceptance criterion (10 projects × 500 beads = 5,000) already meets this for beads, but restate the floor explicitly: **no perf work lands against a fixture smaller than 1,000 records per query shape**. Sessions fixtures, efficacy fixtures, provider/quota history fixtures — all ≥1k rows. Beads fixture stays at 5k; sessions fixture for `ddx-2ceb02fa` should be ≥10k index entries (already specified); efficacy and any new surfaces ≥1k attempts/rows each.

2. **DB backend is on the table.** If in-place optimization (scope, pushdown, lightweight index) cannot hit the target latencies on the fixture sizes above, the user is open to introducing a real data store — candidates named: **axon**, **sqlite**, **postgres**. Do not silently pick one: **raise the decision with the user before choosing a path.** Capture the baseline, capture what in-place optimization achieved, then bring the choice (and the rationale) to the user.

These directives supersede any acceptance criterion that implies "optimize in place no matter what." If the numbers say storage needs to change, that's the outcome — but the user decides the substrate.

**AC §8 — Playwright cross-project test routes wrong path**: `beads-smoke.spec.ts:107` navigates to `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/projects/${PROJECT_ID}/beads` for the "cross-project" test, which is the same per-project URL as the 1s test. A cross-project smoke test should hit the all-beads route (e.g., `/nodes/${NODE_INFO.id}/beads` or whatever the actual cross-project URL is) and mock the `beads` query, not `beadsByProject`. The mock for `beads` exists (lines 76-89) but is never exercised because both tests navigate to the project-scoped path.
**AC §9 — Bead notes cross-reference**: The acceptance criteria require this bead's notes to list `ddx-ad0db8fd` and `ddx-2ceb02fa`. The baseline `.md` and code comments mention them, but no `ddx bead update` call or notes update appears in the diff. If the notes were updated via a separate mechanism not captured in this branch, this finding can be dismissed.

.ddx/executions/20260415T011331-36e59382/prompt.md:5:The embedded 'agent' harness can complete a task successfully (commit produced, tests pass) but then fail to terminate, getting stuck in a compaction retry loop with compaction always failing. The parent execute-loop never observes task_succeeded and hangs forever.

## Observed

Running 'ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --harness agent --model qwen/qwen3-coder-next' against axon-43ed769b (axon auth-core-types bead):

1. qwen3-coder-next ran the task correctly, producing commit 91d4bdb with 636 LOC of types + 109 green tests + clippy clean. The commit was present in the worktree at /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-axon-43ed769b-20260415T005652-ebe7a16a.

2. Instead of terminating, the agent kept generating events. After ~20 minutes the session log was 33 MB (8461+ events, 249 messages) and stuck in:

    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    {"type":"compaction.start",...}
    {"type":"compaction.end",...,"data":{"messages_after":249,"messages_before":249,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
    ...

3. Parent ddx process in __futex_wait. Had to SIGTERM the parent (PID 4141811) and manually fast-forward-merge the worktree commit to land the work.

4. A1 (previous bead) ran the same harness/model and terminated correctly in ~14 min with identical-looking commit shape. The difference was bead size: A2's output was a single 633-line Rust file, vs A1's 350-line SQL + 290-line test file. A2 didn't exceed what A1 did — so the context-limit explanation doesn't fully account for it.

## Expected

- After the agent commits and has nothing more to do, it should emit task_succeeded and terminate
- Compaction should either succeed (messages_after < messages_before) or bail out cleanly, not retry indefinitely
- If the context genuinely cannot be compacted, the agent should report a structured failure and preserve the commits, not spin

## Scope

- Detect mid-turn compaction returning success=false repeatedly and bail with a structured 'compaction_stuck' status
- Bound the total number of compaction attempts in a single turn
- Ensure task termination happens immediately after a successful commit + no pending tool calls
- Add a watchdog at the execute-loop level: kill the child if no commit+progress event is observed within a budget

## Evidence

- Session log: /Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
- Rescued commit: 91d4bdb on axon master
- Parent process: PID 4141811 (killed)

## Acceptance

- [ ] Compaction retry loop is bounded and fails structurally
- [ ] Post-commit termination path is deterministic
- [ ] Watchdog detects and terminates stuck sessions with a preserved-commit outcome
- [ ] Regression test reproduces the stuck state (seeded session with large no-op tool calls)

- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go (classify errors returned
  from the review dispatch into the four taxonomy classes per §12:
  context_overflow, provider_empty, unparseable, transport)

  result_rev via existing bead events; on Nth failure emit terminal
  review-manual-required event and do NOT re-execute primary; a new
  result_rev resets the counter; iterations without a result_rev
  (--no-merge, execution-failed) do NOT consume the budget per FEAT-022
  §14)
- cli/internal/config or per-project config (add a review_max_retries
  field — default 3)
- Tests: each of the four error classes triggers from its distinct
  root cause (empty output, unparseable output, context overflow from
  Stage C1, transport failure); retry counter scoping (result_rev
  change resets, --no-merge does not consume budget); terminal
  review-manual-required event after N failures; bead is NOT closed by
  the reviewer failure path (invariant from
  execute_bead_review_failure_modes_test.go:13 stays green).

Out-of-scope:
- Any change that could auto-close a bead on reviewer failure (§13
  invariant is non-negotiable)
- UI for surfacing review-manual-required beads (separate work)
- Context-overflow escalation to a smaller reviewer model or tighter
  caps on retry (noted as follow-up in FEAT-022, not in scope here)

The terminal 'review-manual-required' event body should include the
failure class, attempt count, and the result_rev being blocked, so
operators can triage without opening multiple bundles.

  field — default 3)

  root cause (empty output, unparseable output, context overflow from
  Stage C1, transport failure); retry counter scoping (result_rev
  change resets, --no-merge does not consume budget); terminal
  review-manual-required event after N failures; bead is NOT closed by
  the reviewer failure path (invariant from
  execute_bead_review_failure_modes_test.go:13 stays green).

- Any change that could auto-close a bead on reviewer failure (§13
  invariant is non-negotiable)
- UI for surfacing review-manual-required beads (separate work)
- Context-overflow escalation to a smaller reviewer model or tighter
  caps on retry (noted as follow-up in FEAT-022, not in scope here)

The terminal 'review-manual-required' event body should include the
failure class, attempt count, and the result_rev being blocked, so
operators can triage without opening multiple bundles.

.ddx/executions/20260415T040630-c7445ab6/prompt.md:5:'ddx agent execute-loop' help says 'execute-loop scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue.' When submitting to a multi-project server, there is no way to say 'run this loop against project X'. The server picks whichever project is the current server context, ignoring the caller's CWD.

## Observed

From CWD /Users/erik/Projects/axon:

  $ ddx agent execute-loop --once --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --json
  {
    "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",  # WRONG
    "state": "running",
    "worker_id": "worker-20260415T011331-d0a4"
  }

I wanted the worker to pick an axon bead. The server ran the worker against the ddx repo instead. There is no --project flag, no env var, nothing in the CLI to redirect the server's project scope.

Currently the only way to target a specific project with execute-loop is --local mode (which spawns a subprocess using the caller's CWD as the project root). This defeats the purpose of having a server in a multi-project deployment.

## Scope

- --project <path> or --project <name> flag on execute-loop, execute-bead, and 'ddx agent run' that tells the server which project_root to use
- Env var fallback: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT or similar
- Default to CWD if unset and no flag
- Server refuses ambiguous/unmatched project names with a clean error
- Integration test: from CWD A, submit a loop targeting project B; assert worker runs in B's project_root

## Why this matters for the user's workflow

Without this, the ONLY way to drive multi-project execution from one shell is --local, which (a) loses the server's queue visibility, (b) ties the parent process to the child, and (c) negates the 'ddx agent execute-loop submits to server by default' ergonomic that execute-loop's --help promises.

## Acceptance

- [ ] --project flag wired through execute-loop/execute-bead/run
- [ ] Env var fallback documented
- [ ] Default behavior: CWD-based project detection
- [ ] Integration test for cross-project submission
- [ ] execute-loop --help updated to describe project targeting

.ddx/executions/20260425T092410-e9991cad/prompt.md:5:Upstream report ("ddx execute-loop: post-merge reviewer silently fails on large prompts", suggested fix #5) flags that even when the reviewer returns content, the markdown verdict extractor mis-parses it: when Claude responds with '### Verdict: APPROVE' the extractor pulls 'BLOCK' from the options header line in the prompt template that the model echoes back, logging '{"verdict":"BLOCK"}' for an actual APPROVE. This silently lets regressions land.

FEAT-022 (parent ddx-0c35470e) is scoped to prompt assembly and size invariants; response parsing is out of scope there. File this as a sibling.

Switch the reviewer contract to a machine-readable JSON object emitted as the model's final response:

  {
    "verdict": "APPROVE" | "REQUEST_CHANGES" | "BLOCK",
    "findings": [
      { "severity": "info"|"warn"|"block", "summary": "…", "location": "path:line?" }
    ],
    "summary": "≤300 char human-readable verdict justification"
  }

Replace the existing markdown extractor with a strict JSON parser:
1. Update the reviewer prompt template (post-collapse: agent.BuildReviewPrompt) to instruct the model to emit ONLY a JSON object matching the schema, fenced as ```json … ``` for robustness against models that won't emit raw JSON.
2. Add internal/agent/review_verdict.go with a typed ReviewVerdict struct and a ParseReviewVerdict([]byte) (ReviewVerdict, error) that strips an optional ```json fence and decodes; reject on unknown verdict values.
3. Replace the regex-based extractor call site in execute_bead_review.go with ParseReviewVerdict; on parse error, emit the existing 'unparseable' review-error class (introduced by ddx-70c1d2e2) — do NOT close the bead.
4. Schema-version the contract: include schema_version: 1 in the prompt; the parser tolerates unknown fields but rejects unknown verdict values.

In-scope files:
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go (prompt template, extractor call site)
- cli/internal/agent/review_verdict.go (new)
- cli/internal/agent/review_verdict_test.go (new)
- prompt template asset under cli/internal/agent/assets/ if separated

Out of scope:
- Bounded retry / error-class taxonomy (ddx-70c1d2e2 owns this; this bead emits 'unparseable' into that taxonomy)
- Prompt size invariants (FEAT-022)
- Grading verdicts (separate bead if needed; this is review-only)

Cross-reference: This bead's `unparseable` emission feeds into ddx-70c1d2e2's bounded-retry logic. ddx-70c1d2e2 should be reviewed once both land to confirm the unparseable retry budget is sane (the upstream report observed real APPROVE verdicts being lost, so unparseable should retry, not terminal-escalate immediately).

This is error-prone and loses the atomicity guarantee that a reopen is a single logical event.\n\n## Scope\n- ddx bead reopen \u003cid\u003e [--reason \u003ctext\u003e] [--append-notes \u003ctext\u003e]\n- Atomically: set status=open, append a reopen event to the event log, optionally append notes, clear claim fields\n- Emit an event of kind=reopen that shows in ddx bead show\n- Works from CLI and from ddx agent execute-loop review-rejection path\n\n## Context\nObserved during axon ADR-018 loop: first attempt merged, opus review rejected, I had to run three ddx bead update commands plus a description rewrite to get the bead back into a ready-for-second-attempt state. An atomic reopen would have been one command.",
.ddx/executions/20260415T045751-302274f4/prompt.md:5:Reopening a closed bead after a failing review currently takes 3-4 separate commands: update --status open, update --notes (append), optionally update --description, optionally update --acceptance. This is error-prone and loses the atomicity guarantee that a reopen is a single logical event.

## Scope
- ddx bead reopen <id> [--reason <text>] [--append-notes <text>]
- Atomically: set status=open, append a reopen event to the event log, optionally append notes, clear claim fields
- Emit an event of kind=reopen that shows in ddx bead show
- Works from CLI and from ddx agent execute-loop review-rejection path

## Context
Observed during axon ADR-018 loop: first attempt merged, opus review rejected, I had to run three ddx bead update commands plus a description rewrite to get the bead back into a ready-for-second-attempt state. An atomic reopen would have been one command.

.ddx/executions/20260415T053655-96cfa2ec/prompt.md:5:execute-loop currently closes a bead on merge. There is no automated post-merge review step validating the commit against the bead's AC. Real-world practice requires a second-opinion agent (typically a higher-capability tier) to produce APPROVE / REQUEST_CHANGES.

## Principle

Review is on-by-default with zero flags. The tool picks a tier >= the implementation tier (defaulting to smart). The user overrides only in exceptional cases.

## Default flow

- Implementation agent runs at current tier (see ddx-dc231144 tier-based escalation)
- On successful merge, tool automatically renders a review prompt via 'ddx bead review' (ddx-fdf031c9)
- Review agent runs at max(impl_tier + 1, smart)
- Review result is structured JSON: { verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK, findings: [...] }
- APPROVE → close bead, attach review evidence
- REQUEST_CHANGES → reopen bead via 'ddx bead reopen' (ddx-44084b3e), append review body, let the tier-escalation policy pick the next attempt
- BLOCK → stop escalating, flag for human

## Rare overrides

- --no-review (skip review entirely — e.g., doc-only beads in a tight loop)
- --review-harness / --review-model (explicit reviewer override for debug)
- Per-bead opt-out via a 'review:skip' label on the bead

## Dependencies

- ddx-fdf031c9 (ddx bead review command) provides the review prompt template
- ddx-44084b3e (ddx bead reopen) provides the atomic reopen primitive
- ddx-518b2690 (plain --output text) makes extracting the review verdict trivial
- ddx-5bac3c6b (provider health probe) feeds the reviewer tier selection

## Context

Observed during axon ADR-018 execute-loop:
- qwen3-coder-next merged A1 with real AC gaps (missing round-trip tests, zero Postgres coverage, scope creep)
- Without review, the bead would have closed silently and the gaps would surface only at epic rollup — much later and much more expensive to fix
- Review took 41s + a few minutes of reading. Cheap relative to the real cost of shipping broken code downstream.
.ddx/executions/20260415T053924-05dbebde/manifest.json:14:    "description": "'ddx agent execute-loop' help says 'execute-loop scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue.' When submitting to a multi-project server, there is no way to say 'run this loop against project X'. The server picks whichever project is the current server context, ignoring the caller's CWD.\n\n## Observed\n\nFrom CWD /Users/erik/Projects/axon:\n\n  $ ddx agent execute-loop --once --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --json\n  {\n    \"project_root\": \"/Users/erik/Projects/ddx\",  # WRONG\n    \"state\": \"running\",\n    \"worker_id\": \"worker-20260415T011331-d0a4\"\n  }\n\nI wanted the worker to pick an axon bead. The server ran the worker against the ddx repo instead. There is no --project flag, no env var, nothing in the CLI to redirect the server's project scope.\n\nCurrently the only way to target a specific project with execute-loop is --local mode (which spawns a subprocess using the caller's CWD as the project root). This defeats the purpose of having a server in a multi-project deployment.\n\n## Scope\n\n- --project \u003cpath\u003e or --project \u003cname\u003e flag on execute-loop, execute-bead, and 'ddx agent run' that tells the server which project_root to use\n- Env var fallback: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT or similar\n- Default to CWD if unset and no flag\n- Server refuses ambiguous/unmatched project names with a clean error\n- Integration test: from CWD A, submit a loop targeting project B; assert worker runs in B's project_root\n\n## Why this matters for the user's workflow\n\nWithout this, the ONLY way to drive multi-project execution from one shell is --local, which (a) loses the server's queue visibility, (b) ties the parent process to the child, and (c) negates the 'ddx agent execute-loop submits to server by default' ergonomic that execute-loop's --help promises.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- [ ] --project flag wired through execute-loop/execute-bead/run\n- [ ] Env var fallback documented\n- [ ] Default behavior: CWD-based project detection\n- [ ] Integration test for cross-project submission\n- [ ] execute-loop --help updated to describe project targeting",
.ddx/executions/20260415T053924-05dbebde/prompt.md:5:'ddx agent execute-loop' help says 'execute-loop scans the current project's execution-ready bead queue.' When submitting to a multi-project server, there is no way to say 'run this loop against project X'. The server picks whichever project is the current server context, ignoring the caller's CWD.

## Observed

From CWD /Users/erik/Projects/axon:

  $ ddx agent execute-loop --once --harness claude --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --json
  {
    "project_root": "/Users/erik/Projects/ddx",  # WRONG
    "state": "running",
    "worker_id": "worker-20260415T011331-d0a4"
  }

I wanted the worker to pick an axon bead. The server ran the worker against the ddx repo instead. There is no --project flag, no env var, nothing in the CLI to redirect the server's project scope.

Currently the only way to target a specific project with execute-loop is --local mode (which spawns a subprocess using the caller's CWD as the project root). This defeats the purpose of having a server in a multi-project deployment.

## Scope

- --project <path> or --project <name> flag on execute-loop, execute-bead, and 'ddx agent run' that tells the server which project_root to use
- Env var fallback: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT or similar
- Default to CWD if unset and no flag
- Server refuses ambiguous/unmatched project names with a clean error
- Integration test: from CWD A, submit a loop targeting project B; assert worker runs in B's project_root

## Why this matters for the user's workflow

Without this, the ONLY way to drive multi-project execution from one shell is --local, which (a) loses the server's queue visibility, (b) ties the parent process to the child, and (c) negates the 'ddx agent execute-loop submits to server by default' ergonomic that execute-loop's --help promises.

## Acceptance

- [ ] --project flag wired through execute-loop/execute-bead/run
- [ ] Env var fallback documented
- [ ] Default behavior: CWD-based project detection
- [ ] Integration test for cross-project submission
- [ ] execute-loop --help updated to describe project targeting

The tool already knows which tiers exist (model-catalog.yaml: cheap/standard/smart) and which harnesses are configured. Given a live probe of each provider's health at attempt time, the loop can auto-escalate on its own.\n\n## Principle\n\nDiscovery over configuration. The user shouldn't write a fallback chain — the tool should figure it out.\n\n## Default policy (no flags)\n\n- Start at **cheap** tier, execute the bead\n- If merge succeeds AND post-review (gap #1) APPROVES → close\n- If anything fails (execution_failed, no_changes after N retries, post_run_check_failed, REQUEST_CHANGES from review) → escalate one tier and retry\n- Chain: cheap → standard → smart → exhausted (preserve, flag for human)\n- Review agent runs at max(impl_tier+1, smart). Tool should do this.\n- qwen3-coder-next merged but opus review rejected — I manually re-invoked with sonnet. Tool should escalate automatically.\n- Resulting CLI invocation in my notes was too complex (—-fallback-chain a/b,c/d,e/f --post-review g/h). User feedback: too many knobs.
